%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# Time-Reversal Property

> **Section Objective:** Learn how time reversal in a discrete-time signal changes its z-transform expression and region of convergence.

## Concepts In This Section

- Time-reversal property
- Substitution \(z \to 1/z\)
- ROC inversion
- Complex-signal conjugate version
- Unit-step drill example

## 1. The starting transform pair

This is the original z-transform pair before any time reversal is applied. Here, \(x[n]\) is the discrete-time sequence defined for integer values of \(n\), \(X[z]\) is its z-transform, and \(z\) is the complex transform variable.

If the original sequence is known through \(X[z]\), the time-reversal property tells us how to transform \(x[-n]\) without recomputing the full summation from scratch.

**Exam trigger:** The problem gives a known pair and asks for the transform of the reversed sequence.

**Common misuse:** Changing \(x[n]\) to \(x[-n]\) in the time domain but leaving \(X[z]\) unchanged in the z-domain — the transform expression must also be updated.

$$x[n] \Longleftrightarrow X[z]$$

## 2. Time reversal in the z-domain

Replacing \(n\) by \(-n\) reflects the sequence across \(n = 0\) in the time domain. In the z-domain, this reflection corresponds exactly to the substitution \(z \mapsto 1/z\).

Symbol meanings: \(x[-n]\) is the time-reversed sequence; \(X[1/z]\) means evaluate the original transform expression at \(1/z\) — not multiply the whole transform by \(1/z\).

**Proof idea in one sentence:** In the z-transform summation, the factor \(z^{-n}\) becomes \(z^{n}\) after replacing \(n \to -n\), and \(z^{n}\) can be written as \((1/z)^{-n}\), which is exactly the summation kernel evaluated at \(1/z\).

**Exam trigger:** Any expression containing \(x[-n]\).

**Most common misuse:** Writing \(1/X[z]\) instead of \(X[1/z]\). These are completely different operations.

$$x[-n] \Longleftrightarrow X[1/z]$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## 3. Complex-signal version

When the signal is complex-valued, the time-reversal rule gains an extra conjugation step. Here \(^*\) denotes the complex conjugate.

Read the formula carefully: the time-domain signal is both reversed and conjugated, giving \(x^*[-n]\). In the z-domain, the transform is conjugated after substituting \(1/z^*\) — note that the argument is \(1/z^*\), not \(1/z\).

**Minimal example phrase:** This version applies to expressions like \(x^*[-n]\), not to every ordinary real-valued time reversal. For real signals, the ordinary rule \(x[-n] \Longleftrightarrow X[1/z]\) is sufficient.

**Exam trigger:** The problem explicitly includes a complex-valued sequence or the expression \(x^*[-n]\).

**Common misuse:** Applying the conjugate version to a real sequence when the ordinary rule is enough, or forgetting to conjugate the argument \(z\) inside \(X^*[\cdot]\).

$$x^{*}[-n] \Longleftrightarrow X^{*}[1/z^{*}]$$

## 4. ROC inversion

Time reversal does not only change the algebraic expression — it also inverts the region of convergence. A right-sided sequence with an outside-circle ROC becomes a left-sided sequence with an inside-circle ROC after reversal.

Specifically: if the original ROC is \(|z| > |\gamma|\) (outside a circle of radius \(|\gamma|\)), the reversed ROC is \(|z| < 1/|\gamma|\) (inside a circle of radius \(1/|\gamma|\)). Here \(\gamma\) is the boundary parameter from the original transform.

**Minimal example:** If the original ROC is \(|z| > 2\), the reversed ROC is \(|z| < 1/2\).

**Exam trigger:** The question asks for both the z-transform expression and the ROC after time reversal.

**Most common misuse:** Substituting \(1/z\) into \(X[z]\) correctly but keeping the old ROC inequality direction — the inequality must flip and the radius must be reciprocated.

$$\text{If the ROC is } |z|>|\gamma|,\text{ then after time reversal the ROC is } |z|<\frac{1}{|\gamma|}.$$

## 5. Drill 5.8: unit-step example

This is the textbook drill result for applying the time-reversal property to the unit step \(u[n]\).

**Reading path:**
1. Start from the known unit-step pair: \(u[n] \Longleftrightarrow \frac{z}{z-1}\), ROC \(|z| > 1\).
2. Time-reverse to get \(u[-n]\): substitute \(z \mapsto 1/z\) in the expression.
3. The substitution gives \(\frac{(1/z)}{(1/z)-1} = \frac{1/z}{(1-z)/z} = \frac{1}{1-z} = -\frac{1}{z-1}\).
4. Invert the ROC: \(|z| > 1\) becomes \(|z| < 1\).

The final result is \(-1/(z-1)\) with ROC \(|z| < 1\).

**Exam trigger:** A familiar sequence appears with \(-n\) in the argument.

**Most common misuse:** Giving the correct expression \(-1/(z-1)\) but writing \(|z| > 1\) instead of \(|z| < 1\) for the ROC.

$$u[-n] \Longleftrightarrow -\frac{1}{z-1},\quad \text{ROC: } |z|<1$$

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Starting pair: \(x[n] \Longleftrightarrow X[z]\); time reversal gives \(x[-n] \Longleftrightarrow X[1/z]\) — substitute \(1/z\) into the expression, do not invert the whole transform.
- ROC inversion rule: \(|z|>|\gamma| \Rightarrow |z|<1/|\gamma|\) — both the inequality direction and the boundary radius change.
- Complex-signal version: \(x^*[-n] \Longleftrightarrow X^*[1/z^*]\) — applies only when the signal is explicitly complex-valued.
- Drill 5.8 result: \(u[-n] \Longleftrightarrow -1/(z-1)\), ROC \(|z|<1\) — the ROC is inside the unit circle, not outside.

*Next, we use z-transform properties to handle convolution.*
